item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Might want to be more specific here so the author knows what they'd be agreeing to? "The concepts of the article" could be anything from a small detail to the entire anomaly, an "an off-site work" could be anything from a side wiki project to a graduate dissertation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would you mind if I referenced the concepts of this article in an off-site work?

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: timer expired, edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

Considering that this is a temporal anomaly, I propose linking to SCP-1780. My rationale for this is that, since this is a temporal anomaly, the Temporal Division would be involved in its containment.

Editing this:

The house containing SCP-2780 is to be occupied by Foundation security officers to prevent unauthorized access.

To this:

The house containing SCP-2780 is to be occupied by Foundation security officers belonging to the Temporal Division to prevent unauthorized access.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still like this three years later. A little bit heavy on the details but the concept is enjoyable and well-executed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love messing with time, especially when (in my opinion, at least) it makes sense. But I also find it interesting if this anomaly is completely perpetuated by the Foundation. Imagine: there is no real Sarah Fuhlvia. In fact, as soon as they realized this (in the year 2089, I suppose), they had a panic. And what happened is that they made up Sarah, and gave an agent that Alias and told her to go into the house and follow orders, and so the entire anomaly is completed by the Foundation, and then we hit a paradox (if we haven't already): what if Sarah never went out of commission? What if, while they were listening, Sarah never left the house. And then they have to keep the agent doing that job until she dies, because that's how they heard it went. They can't stop doing this thing that they(?) started because they got involved in the past and heard how it went and don't want to (possibly can't) alter the timeline.

I love paradoxes, and this is my headcanon. Extraordinarily enthusiastic +1, this… this could be a movie. (Yes, I know of The Lake House)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *LOVE* this!! The second SCP I wish I could upvote twice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not a fan of long article, but this actually drew me in with its elaborate scheme and (possibility because I'm a medical person) the description of future drugs. Well done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really enoyed this, and not just because I'm a sucker for time travel and science fiction.

EDIT: I also love your attention to detail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your cat's name is Gustav Adolf II, King and Emperor by the Grace of God of Sweden and her associated territories? Shit. Naming conventions with dogs are much simpler. For example, my dog's name is "Marlin." No fancy titles or nothing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: GUS[TAV ADOLF II, KING AND EMPEROR BY THE GRACE OF GOD OF SWEDEN AND HER ASSOCIATED TERRITORIES, THE CAT] DON'T NEED A REASON TO FUCK UP YOUR BANDWIDTH

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why are there pictures of a cat in the files?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found this unnecessarily long overall (also moose is a giant hypocrite). It's still great though. A lot of nice detail work — funny, entertaining, and highly believable.

also you should put that cat in pls

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. This was a greatly enjoyable read. The story you put together around a very basic temporal anomaly was entertaining and well thought-out in its own right, and the future worldbuilding you've done mainly through vague dialogue about a social breakdown and over-the-counter drugs doesn't overdo it, yet still makes it memorable in comparison to other similarly ill-defined crapsack futures. I want to see more anomalies treated this way — less as a narrative in themselves and more as a vehicle by which a story can be told.
  2. dude is that your actual house and address

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I had to go over that part a couple times, but it is grammatically correct, at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love all these recent TN skips.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm apparently going to be the designated party-pooper for this, but I'm not digging this. Maybe it's me being Grammar Fascist, but there are mistakes and odd wording choices that made reading like driving down a highway with occasional speed bumps. The "Lake House but with drugs" aspect is ok, though not awe-inspiring, which leaves me hovering pretty much in the middle of neutral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted; very fun skip.

My interpretation is that at some point, Sahrah Fulvia has already been replaced by the 2090's Foundation. They and our Foundation are working in a double-blind sort of manner to contain the anomaly, and this future Foundation's staff feels our Foundation's efforts are seriously lacking. They can't divulge the replacement of Fulvia because it might disrupt the timeline somehow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. This is fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know why, but this is downright fun to read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. Good mix of futurism and mundane.

Sahrah Fulvia, about) SCP-2780's secondary effect

But something went wrong here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, it's just a bit dry, which is saying something for the tone of this site really. I found myself drifting a bit when I was trying to read it. You wouldn't need to do the whole thing in interviews, just a few excerpts (or even excerpts of any notes passed between them, something like that?) would break the clinical tone for a bit and introduce a bit more human interest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, I thought about it, and then I decided to do the thing that would normally have been done in interviews (the whole conversation with Tucker) just in prose. Interviews can be touchy as hell to do, and I just wasn't feeling it on this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was 'eh' until the nod to 'T' at which point I upvoted immediately for my own reasons. I like the idea but I found it a bit boring to read. You could maybe spice it up a bit with some actual interview excerpts? Still, deserved the upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awesome job! Now I see why you were asking about medications that would likely become OTC in the future in chat…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of the reasons I enjoy temporal skips so much is because they usually force the Foundation to propagate the skip. Here we have the Foundation actively assisting in the propagation of a skip because it's their only choice if they want to preserve the rest of reality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do love a good cross-time caper. The time-and-location-specific temporal communication back and forth reminded me a whole lot of The Lake House ("You're gonna think I'm crazy!" - Keanu's best ever line delivery, btw) but who cares, this was engaging and appropriately detailed from beginning to end. Plus One!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this is based on observations of SCP-2780 that include recordings of her frequent comments on the Foundation and its inefficacious handling of the containment effort, as well as several epithets of a personal and often sexual nature

"HEY, FOUNDATION! YOU CAN GO SUCK MY [EXPLETIVE] AND [EXPLETIVE] YOUR MOM"

I dunno if I told you this when I reviewed your draft in chat last night, but I greatly enjoy this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was the most fun thing to write though. And while I couldn't tell you why it has to be that way, it makes sense that the Foundation would a) determine that that is the case and then b) mess with that as little as possible to avoid destroying the anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the whole dresser thing seemed a little to specific and a bit confusing with the north east west stuff for me but a good concept

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean, the setting, the advanced pharmaceutical technology coming through it, and the fact that whoever's on the other end almost certainly told Ms. Tucker what the date was strongly suggests that it's 75 years in the future. There are complications such as "how do we know it isn't an alternate universe?" or "how do we know it isn't an alternate future?" or something like that, but Occam's Razor suggests that it's from the future, and the Foundation is going to have to behave in the most conservative fashion possible: namely, if this is the actual, honest-to-God future, and if temporal paradoxes are possible, they'll have to contain this thing very carefully to avoid disruption of the timeline.

Which requires cooperation from the Foundation in the future, which is…bizarrely not presenting itself…

item.title: OUTTAKES FROM THE PHOTO SHOOT FOR THIS SCP
item.content:encoded:
An agent from a rival organization entered the rear bedroom containing SCP-2780; Foundation agents attempted to contain him
only to find he was there for legitimate snuggles and sleepy-poos.
only to find he was there for legitimate snuggles and sleepy-poos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So wait.
How does the foundation know this is 75 years in the future?
No vote for now, because it seems like an interesting idea, but at the same time, it's very confusing.
